verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: The Son is here [Ignat.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Eph. vii.] declared to be γεννητὸς as man and ἀγέννητος as God, for this is clearly shown to be the meaning from the parallel clauses.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Such language is not in accordance with later theological definitions, which carefully distinguished between γενητός and γεννητός between ἀγένητος and ἀγέννητος; so that γενητός, ἀγένητος respectively denied and affirmed the eternal existence, being equivalent to κτιστός, ἄκτιστος, while γεννητός, ἀγέννητος described certain ontological relations, whether in time or in eternity.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: In the later theological language, therefore, the Son was γεννητός even in his Godhead.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Damasc. de Fid.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: 8 [where he draws the conclusion that only the Father is ἀγέννητος, and only the Son γεννητός].

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: There can be little doubt however, that Ignatius wrote γεννητός καὶ ἀγέννητος, though his editors frequently alter it into γενητὸς καὶ ἀγένητος.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: For (1) the Greek ms. still retains the double [Greek nun] ν, though the claims of orthodoxy would be a temptation to scribes to substitute the single ν.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: And to this reading also the Latin genitus et ingenitus points.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: On the other hand it cannot be concluded that translators who give factus et non factus had the words with one ν, for this was after all what Ignatius meant by the double ν, and they would naturally render his words so as to make his orthodoxy apparent.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: (2) When Theodoret writes γεννητὸς ἐξ ἀγεννήτου, it is clear that he, or the person before him who first substituted this reading, must have read γεννητὸς καὶ ἀγέννητος, for there would be no temptation to alter the perfectly orthodox γενητὸς καὶ ἀγένητος, nor (if altered) would it have taken this form.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: (3) When the interpolator substitutes ὁ μόνος ἄληθινὸς Θεὸς ὁ ἀγέννητος…τοῦ δὲ μονογονοῦς πατῂρ καὶ γεννήτωρ, the natural inference is that he too, had the forms in double ν, which he retained, at the same time altering the whole run of the sentence so as not to do violence to his own doctrinal views; see Bull Def.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: (4) The quotation in Athanasius is more difficult.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: I believe, however, that the argument of Athanasius decides in favour of the νν.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Elsewhere he insists repeatedly on the distinction between κτίζειν and γεννᾶν, justifying the use of the latter term as applied to the divinity of the Son, and defending the statement in the Nicene Creed γεννητὸν ἐκ τῆς οὐσίας τοῦ πατρὸς τὸν υἱὸν ὁμοούσιον (De Synod.

verses.text: In the passage before us, ib. §§ 46, 47 (p.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: 607), he is defending the use of homousios at Nicæa, notwithstanding that it had been previously rejected by the council which condemned Paul of Samosata, and he contends that both councils were orthodox, since they used homousios in a different sense.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: As a parallel instance he takes the word ἀγέννητος which like homousios is not a scriptural word, and like it also is used in two ways, signifying either (1) Τὸ ὂν μεν, μήτε δὲ γεννηθὲν μήτε ὅλως ἔχον τὸν αἴτιον, or (2) Τὸ ἄκτιστον.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: In the former sense the Son cannot be called ἀγέννητος, in the latter he may be so called.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Both uses, he says, are found in the fathers.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Of the latter he quotes the passage in Ignatius as an example; of the former he says, that some writers subsequent to Ignatius declare ἕν τὸ ἀγέννητον ὁ πατὴρ, καὶ εἶς ὁ ἐξ αὐτου υἱὸς γνήσιος, γέννημα αληθίνον κ.τ.λ.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: He maintains that both are orthodox, as having in view two different senses of the word ἀγέννητον , and the same, he argues, is the case with the councils which seem to take opposite sides with regard to homousios.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: It is clear from this passage, as Zahn truly says, that Athanasius is dealing with one and the same word throughout; and, if so, it follows that this word must be ἀγέννητον, since ἀγένητον would be intolerable in some places.

verses.text: If then Athanasius who read γεννητὸς καὶ ἀγέννητος in Ignatius, there is absolutely no authority for the spelling with one ν.

verses.text: The earlier editors (Voss, Ussher, Cotelier, etc.), printed it as they found it in the ms.; but Smith substituted the forms with the single ν, and he has been followed more recently by Hefele, Dressel, and some other.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Yet notwithstanding the confusion into which later transcribers have thus thrown the subject, it is still possible to ascertain the main facts respecting the usage of the two forms.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: The distinction between the two terms, as indicated by their origin, is that ἀγένητος denies the creation, and ἀγέννητος the generation or parentage.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Both are used at a very early date; e.g. ἀγένητος by Parmenides in Clem.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: In Christian writers also there is no reason to suppose that the distinction was ever lost, though in certain connexions the words might be used convertibly.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Whenever, as here in Ignatius, we have the double ν where we should expect the single, we must ascribe the fact to the indistinctness or incorrectness of the writer’s theological conceptions, not to any obliteration of the meaning of the terms themselves.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: To this early father for instance the eternal γέννησις of the Son was not a distinct theological idea, though substantially he held the same views as the Nicene fathers respecting the Person of Christ.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: The following passages from early Christian writers will serve at once to show how far the distinction was appreciated, and to what extent the Nicene conception prevailed in ante-Nicene Christianity; Justin Apol. ii.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: From the above passages it will appear that Ante-Nicene writers were not indifferent to the distinction of meaning between the two words; and when once the orthodox Christology was formulated in the Nicene Creed in the words γεννηθέντα οὐ ποιηθέντα, it became henceforth impossible to overlook the difference.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: The Son was thus declared to be γεννητός but not γενητός.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: While the orthodox party clung to the homousios as enshrining the doctrine for which they fought, they had no liking for the terms ἀγέννητος and γεννητός as applied to the Father and the Son respectively, though unable to deny their propriety, because they were affected by the Arians and applied in their own way.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: To the orthodox mind the Arian formula οὐκ ἦν πρὶν γεννηθήναι or some Semiarian formula hardly less dangerous, seemed always to be lurking under the expression Θεὸς γεννητός as applied to the Son.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Hence the language of Epiphanius Hær. lxxiii.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: 19: “As you refuse to accept our homousios because though used by the fathers, it does not occur in the Scriptures, so will we decline on the same grounds to accept your ἀγέννητος .”

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Similarly Basil c.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: Eunom. i., iv., and especially ib. further on, in which last passage he argues at great length against the position of the heretics, εἰ ἀγέννητος, φασὶν, ὁ πατήρ, γεννητὸς δὲ ὁ υἱός, οὐ τῆς αὐτῆς οὐσίας.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: This fully explains the reluctance of the orthodox party to handle terms which their adversaries used to endanger the homousios.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: But, when the stress of the Arian controversy was removed, it became convenient to express the Catholic doctrine by saying that the Son in his divine nature was γέννητος but not γένητος.

verses.book: The First Ecumenical Council: The First Council of Nice.
verses.text: And this distinction is staunchly maintained in later orthodox writers, e.g. John of Damascus, already quoted in the beginning of this Excursus.
